answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use a dictionary to count each character. Traverse the string once and update counts. If the character is not present, initialize it to 0 and then increment.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: First build a frequency dictionary. Then scan the string again in order and return the first character whose frequency is 1.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: Two-pass approach: first count, then scan.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use a dictionary to store numbers and their indices. For each element, compute target - current value and check if it already exists in the dictionary.

answers.author: System Hint
answers.content: If needed in seen: return (seen[needed], i)

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Check if both strings have the same length. Then count characters in both using dictionaries and compare the two maps.

answers.author: Champak Roy
answers.content: Use the sorted version of each word as a key. Store words with the same key in a dictionary list. Finally return all grouped values.
